card.intuition: Each hash table slot holds a linked list (or array) of all entries that hash to that slot. Insertion appends to the chain; lookup traverses it. With a good hash function and load factor α < 1, the expected chain length is O(α) ≈ O(1).
card.analogy: A post office with numbered mailboxes: multiple letters can arrive for the same box number (collision), so each box holds a stack of letters. Finding your letter means checking your box number and scanning through the stack.
card.time_complexity: O(1 + α) expected per operation where α = n/m is the load factor
card.space_complexity: O(n + m) — n entries + m bucket pointers
when_to_use: When load factor may exceed 1 (chaining handles it gracefully; open addressing cannot)
When deletions are frequent — no tombstone complexity
Java's HashMap, most textbook hash table implementations
gotchas: Chain length is O(n) worst case if all keys hash to the same bucket — use a good hash function
Java 8+ converts chains to red-black trees when chain length > 8 — O(log n) worst case
Pointer overhead makes chaining cache-unfriendly compared to open addressing

exercise.prompt: Implement delete for a separate-chaining hash table.
hints: Once you find the entry at index i in the chain, remove it with chain.pop(i)
exercise.test_code:
t = ChainingTable(4)
t.put('a',1); t.put('b',2); t.put('c',3)
assert t.get('a') == 1
t.delete('b')
try: t.get('b'); assert False
except KeyError: pass
assert t.get('a') == 1  # others unaffected
assert t.get('c') == 3
try: t.delete('missing'); assert False
except KeyError: pass
print('PASS')
